act: Supreme Court & High Court Judgments Dataset
description: Distillation of major Supreme Court rulings, OpenNyAI annotations, and ILDC legal questions to provide contextual case-law to static acts.

sections.title: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
sections.text: The Supreme Court ruled that while Parliament has wide powers to amend the Constitution, it cannot alter its 'basic structure'.
sections.simplified: The Parliament cannot change the core fundamental features (like democracy, secularism) of the Constitution.

sections.title: Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India
sections.text: The Supreme Court unanimously held that the Right to Privacy is a fundamental right under Article 21 of the Constitution.
sections.simplified: Privacy is your fundamental right. The government or private entities cannot illegally track you or steal your personal data.

sections.title: Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan
sections.text: Laid down the 'Vishaka Guidelines' to prevent sexual harassment of women at workplaces, which later formed the basis of the POSH Act.
sections.simplified: Established strict rules against workplace sexual harassment, making employers responsible for employee safety.

sections.title: Arrest without Warrant Rights (D.K. Basu Guidelines)
sections.text: Supreme court guidelines in D.K. Basu v. State of W.B. (1997): Police must wear name tags, inform rights to arrestee, notify a relative within 12 hours, and conduct medical examinations.
sections.simplified: If arrested, you have the right to know why, have a relative informed within 12 hours, and get a medical examination every 48 hours to prevent police brutality.

sections.title: Delay in FIR Registration (OpenNyAI)
sections.text: Lalita Kumari v. Govt. of UP (2014): Registration of FIR is mandatory under Section 154 CrPC if the information discloses commission of a cognizable offence. No preliminary inquiry is permissible in such a situation.
sections.simplified: The Police CANNOT refuse to file an FIR if you report a serious (cognizable) crime. They must register it immediately. If they refuse, you can approach the SP or file online.

sections.title: Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India
sections.text: Decriminalized consensual homosexual activity by striking down parts of Section 377 of the IPC that criminalized 'unnatural offences'.
sections.simplified: Consensual same-sex relationships between adults are legal in India. Discrimination based on sexual orientation is unconstitutional.
